coredata.dc:description: Semiconductor photocatalysts are promising for Oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and also for reducing organic pollutants in wastewater. However, their limited activity under UV light and the fast recombination of electron-hole pairs, hinder their utilization in wastewater treatment. To address these challenges a novel photocatalyst based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) supported Zinc–Cobalt Oxide (ZnCo2O4) was developed, which demonstrated enhanced photocatalytic efficiency. Experimental findings indicated that 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 exhibited superior photocatalytic performance compared to ZnCO2O4 and CQDs/ZnCO2O4 composites materials, achieving over 100 and 96 % decomposition of methylene blue (MB) and methylene orange (MO) dyes after 50 and 140 min under UV light. The enhanced performance of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 can be attributed to the charge transfer rate and synergistic effect between the coupled CQDs and ZnCo2O4. Moreover, studies with radical scavengers reveal that •O2, •OH, and h+ play crucial roles in the breakdown of MB and MO dyes. Furthermore, 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 displayed very low overpotential of 220 mV at 100 mA cm−2 for OER activity with a robust durability for 250 h at 500 mA cm−2.

(a, d) Absorption spectral changes, (b, e), photodegradation curves, (c, f) photodegradation reaction kinetic linear simulation curves under UV light irradiation (a, b, c) for MB and (d, e, f) MO with the photocatalysts of pure ZnCo2O4, 0.2 CQDs/ZnCo2O4, 0.5 CQDs/ZnFe2O4 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 and 0.1 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs, (g, h) Active species trapping experiments using the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs as the photocatalyst under UV light irradiation MB (5 mg L−1), MO (5 mg L−1), and (i) cycling stability of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs for the degradation of MB dye.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Semiconductor photocatalysts are promising for Oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and also for reducing organic pollutants in wastewater. However, their limited activity under UV light and the fast recombination of electron-hole pairs, hinder their utilization in wastewater treatment. To address these challenges a novel photocatalyst based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) supported Zinc–Cobalt Oxide (ZnCo2O4) was developed, which demonstrated enhanced photocatalytic efficiency. Experimental findings indicated that 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 exhibited superior photocatalytic performance compared to ZnCO2O4 and CQDs/ZnCO2O4 composites materials, achieving over 100 and 96 % decomposition of methylene blue (MB) and methylene orange (MO) dyes after 50 and 140 min under UV light. The enhanced performance of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 can be attributed to the charge transfer rate and synergistic effect between the coupled CQDs and ZnCo2O4. Moreover, studies with radical scavengers reveal that •O2, •OH, and h+ play crucial roles in the breakdown of MB and MO dyes. Furthermore, 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 displayed very low overpotential of 220 mV at 100 mA cm−2 for OER activity with a robust durability for 250 h at 500 mA cm−2.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid industrial development has resulted in a significant number of effluent pollutants. Effluents comprise complex constituents such as excessive concentration of refractory organic contaminants and high degree of chrominance with low biodegradability. These dangerous pollutants have a detrimental effect on environment, ecological wellbeing, and human health [1,2]. Therefore, wastewater treatment and efficiently complying it with the ecological discharge standards is challenging in industrialization and urbanization [3,4]. Current wastewater treatment techniques include adsorption, electrochemical processing, sludge activation, ozonation, ion exchange, membrane treatment, and photocatalytic degradation. Though all these techniques are considered sustainable and eco-friendly [5,6], photocatalytic degradation is suggested more effective than the others due to its facility, tunability, efficiency, and more importantly, accessibility of the pollutants-free (secondary pollutants) effluents after treatment [7–10].

Of many available photocatalysts, nanostructured semiconductor photocatalysts are regarded as an excellent catalyst for wastewater treatment and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) since they offer nontoxic and chemical robust structures [1,11]. For instance, Zinc-cobalt oxide (ZnCo2O4) is a typical cobalt spinel structure that has a narrow bandgap of 2.6 eV, nontoxic, and is abundant on earth. ZnCo2O4 has emerged as a promising bifunctional catalyst for wastewater treatment and OER [12–14]. Recently, it has been used in the photocatalytic conversion of CO2 to CO [15,16] and the degradation of organic contaminants [17]. However, the activity of pure ZnCo2O4 was limited by its low photostability, low quantum efficiency, rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and low activity and stability for OER [18–20]. To boost the efficiency of ZnCo2O4 photocatalytic catalyst, the incorporation of another catalyst to design heterostructure catalyst is needed to separate photogenerated electron-hole pairs in wastewater treatment and enhancing its performance in OER [14,19,21].

On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be used as constituents in catalysts. They are a novel class of nanomaterials showcasing superior features, e.g., solid electron conductivity, nontoxicity, low cost, smaller particle size, etc. [22,23]. CQDs are classified as quasi-spherical nanoparticles (NPs) with an average diameter smaller than 10 nm (nm) [16,22]. Their structure is amorphous or crystalline, with carbon rings including sp2 carbon clusters or diamond-like rings composed of sp carbon. For example, the synergistic effects from coupled TiO2 with CQDs inhibits charge recombination and increases the light absorption capacity [24]. CQDs-modified semiconductor materials have recently been investigated for various photocatalytic and electrical applications, including CQDs/BiOI, CQDs/ZnFe2O4, and CQDs/CuO, etc. [25–27] Numerous methods such as co-precipitation, microwave, hydrothermal, and ultrasonication are employed to synthesize CQDs/semiconductor MSs [28–31]. Ultrasonication method is identified as the most efficient technique to fabricate CQDs/semiconductor MSs. This approach promotes reactant and product dispersion and speeds up the chemical reaction [32]. From the literature [31,33,34], photocatalysts produced via ultrasonication exhibited significantly greater photocatalytic activity.

In this work, we prepared CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs heterostructure and employed it as an efficient bifunctional catalyst in OER and wastewater treatment. The as-prepared CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs heterostructure displayed an enhanced performance in wastewater treatment of organic dyes (MB and MO) when compared to single catalysts. We observed that the enhanced performance was due to charge redistribution and synergistic effects between coupled CQDs and ZnCo2O4. A structural photodegradation mechanism is proposed for CQDs/ZnCo2O4 demonstrating consistent high photodegradation rates and robust stability over four consecutive cycles without any change in crystallinity. Moreover, 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 displayed very low overpotential of 220 mV at 100 mA cm−2 for OER activity with a robust durability for 250 h at 500 mA cm−2. These results are supposed to pave a way for the development of enhanced bifunctional OER and waste water treatment catalysts.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate Co(NO3)2·6H2O), trimisic acid, Methylene blue (MB), ethylene glycol, 1,3 propanediol and ethanol (95%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent limited company [M1], and used as received and without any further purification.

A plant (fresh small bamboo shoots) was purchased from the supermarket and grinded thoroughly in a machine. 0.2 g of the grinded plant was added to 35 ml of UPW. The solution was transferred to a 50 ml stainless steel autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the liquid was collected and centrifuged to remove large particles. The liquid was dialyzed for 100 h and then dried in a freezer for the black-brown powder.

ZnCo2O4 was prepared using the solvothermal method. Typically, 0.5 mmol of trimesic acid was completely dissolved in a 25 ml ethylene glycol mixture and 10 ml of 1, 3 propanediol solvent. Then 1 mmol of (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and 2 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6H2O) were added to the above solution and stirred until completely dissolved; Following that, the clear solution was transferred to a 50 ml autoclave and kept at 160 °C for 24 h. After cooling, the residue was washed several times with absolute ethanol and dried in a vacuum for 24 h. Finally, the precursor was calcined at 550 °C for 2 h at 10 °C in a muffle furnace to obtain ZnCo2O4.

Four separate beakers were filled with 0.2, 0.50, 0.7, and 1 g of CQDs powder. Each beaker was filled with 0.1 g ZnCo2O4 powder and 20 ml ethanol solution. The four liquid suspensions were transferred to four porcelain boats and dried in a fuming cupboard after being ultrasonically mixed for 5 h. The porcelain boats were then transferred to a muffle furnace and calcined at 300 °C for 4 h. Grey-black particles were collected and rinsed many times with water and ethanol before being dried overnight in an oven for further analysis (see Fig. 1).

XRD analysis determined the crystalline structure of the prepared pure ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs. Fig. 2 a shows various distinct peaks at 2θ = 18.95°, 31.21°, 36.80°, 38.49°, 44.73°, 55.57°, 59.28°, 65.14°, and 77.21°, which correspond to the hkl values (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), and (533) of ZnCo2O4 with a spinel cubic structure according to (JCPDS NO 23–1339) [15]. Furthermore, there is no additional peak at 2θ = 26° in the synthesized.

CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs material; this may be owing to the material's high dispersion concentration [35]. The observation that the positions of the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 diffraction peaks at 2θ do not vary indicates that the addition of CQDs during synthesis does not affect the crystal structure of pure ZnCo2O4 [1]. Moreover, the thermal properties of ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs were determined using thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). It can be seen that there is a slight mass loss for the pure ZnCo2O4 materials from 0 to 100 °C, which was attributed to the volatilization of a small number of residual water groups in the materials (Fig. 2b), while the weight loss occurring in the range of 200–500 °C in the MSs material is ascribed to the oxidation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which was investigated under nitrogen (N2) environment to prevent oxidation by oxygen, it is essential to note that pure ZnCo2O4 does not exhibit this phase weight loss, and there is no discernible weight loss above 500 °C. The TGA analysis indicates that the 0.7 g CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs comprise doped CQDs. The weight loss for the 0.7 g CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs was calculated to be 11.52%.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to determine the presence of functional groups in the prepared CQDs/ZnCo2O4. The FT-IR spectra of CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs are shown in Fig. 2c. Band positions in the spectrum determined the vibration frequencies of the various chemical linkages in the MSss. The broadband at 2400 cm−1 is attributed to O–H stretching, indicating water molecules' existence. The strong and abrupt peaks at 580 and 660 cm−1 are attributed to the metal-oxygen bond stretching vibrational modes of Co–O and Zn–O, respectively, leading to the development of the ZnCo2O4 spinel structure [36]. The newly formed absorption bands at 1800 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1 coincide with the stretching vibrations of CO and COOH correspondingly the same phenomenon is observed the reported work [37,38].

Raman spectroscopy was used to obtain a typical spectra range of 100–1870 cm-1, revealing changes in composition, structure, and lattice disorder, as shown in Fig. 2d. To achieve the optimum spinel structure, the Raman line must follow the polarization criteria for the five Raman active phonon modes (A₁g + Eg + 2 g) [39]. In this experiment, the stretching vibration peak recorded at ∼183, ∼483, ∼518, and ∼697 cm-1 match to the F₂g, E₂g, F₂g, and A₁g models of ZnCo₂0₄ nanostructures. This demonstrates that all samples are epitaxial, with Co3+ and/or Zn³+ replacing Zn2+ at the octahedral B-sites to maintain equilibrium.

Oxygen vacancy caused by polarization leakage or partial breakdown of the polarization selection rules resulting from local flaws, as previously discussed in the literature [40]. The sample contains sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons, as indicated by two peaks at 1315 cm−1 and 1635 cm−1, which correspond to the G and D bands of carbons, respectively [41]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique was used to characterize the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 sample composition and precise elemental oxidation states. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2e) demonstrates that these constituent elements, which include Zn, Co, O, and C, coexist. The peak fitting of Co2p is shown in Fig. 2g. As can be seen, the Co2p spectrum has two distinct intensity peaks. The peak at low binding energy was ascribed to Co2p3/2, whereas the peak at high binding energy was ascribed to Co2p3/1. The characteristic peaks fitted using the Gauss approach corresponded to various cobalt valences. The intensity peaks at 779.24/794.57 eV were similar to those reported for Co2+ by Xu et colleagues [42]. Compared to the Co3+ ion, Co4+ has a higher positive charge and a lower electron cloud density, implying that the binding energy of the 2p electrons should also be more significant [43]. As a result, the binding energy of Co3+ ions is lower than that of Co4+ ions. Thus, the 778.047/793.434 eV intensity peaks may be attributed to Co3+ ions, whereas the 780.312/803.838 eV intensity peaks can be attributed to Co4+ ions [44]. The Zn 2p spectra (Fig. 2f) reveal two prominent peaks at Binding Energies of 1021.8 and 1045.0 eV, corresponding to the Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2, respectively, indicating the presence of Zn2+ in the MSs [45] Fig. 2h demonstrates that the O 1s peaks at 529.6 and 531.3 eV correlate to oxygen species in ZnCo2O4 [46]. As illustrated in Fig. 2i–C 1s is divided into three distinct peaks. The peaks at 284.5 eV, 286.01 eV, and 287.8 eV are attributed to the C–C, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively, indicating the presence of some novel functional groups in CDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs coated with CQDs.

FESEM was used to investigate the morphologies and microstructures of ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 samples, and representative images are shown in (Fig. 3 a and b). Typical microsphere FESEM images (Fig. 3a and b) display the general morphology of the as-prepared ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 samples, The addition of CQDs to the surface of ZnCo2O4 results in small NPs but has no effect on the morphology of the ZnCo2O4 material after annealing.

The broad surface morphological characteristics of pure CQDs and CQDs/ZnFe2O4 MSs were studied using TEM HRTEM. Pure CQDs revealed a homogeneous spherical particle morphology with varying particle sizes (Fig. 3c and d). Fig. 3e and f shows low magnification TEM images of 0.70 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 at various magnifications. As illustrated in Fig. 3e and f 0.70 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 exhibits a microsphere-like morphology with a well-dispersed structure, which agrees with the SEM image in Fig. 3b. HRTEM images of 0.70 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 are shown in Fig. 3g it shows lattice fringes of 0.258 nm that are well matched to the d (311) spacing of the ZnCo2O4 structure, respectively, and (SAED) images with highlighted crystal planes in the inset in Fig. 3g demonstrate the polycrystalline nature of the 0.70 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 phase.

To determine the composition distributions of CQDs/ZnCo2O4 samples, scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and elemental mapping were performed (Fig. 3h). Its related energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) mapping data unequivocally verify the uniform distributions of the constituent Zn, Co, O, and C in the MSs, Fig. S1. Additionally, CQDs/ZCo2O4 nanospheres have a stoichiometric ratio of four elements, summarized in (Table S1). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of bare ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs was determined using nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms. All are classified as IV-type isotherms with H4-type according to the IUPAC classification, indicating the presence of mesoporous structure (Fig. 4 a). According to the analysis, the specific surface areas of 0.2CQDs/ZnCo2O4, 0.5CQDs/ZnCo2O4, 0.7CQDs/ZnCo2O4, and 1CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs are 20.12, 27.1, 45.15, and 35.15 m2/g, respectively, whereas the specific surface area of pure ZnCo2O4 and CQDs 15.25 and 10.12 m2g-1 (Fig. 4 c).

As can be seen, the specific surface of CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs material efficiently increases in the presence of CQDs. According to the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) pore-size distribution (Fig. 4b), the large pore volume and average pore size of the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSss are approximately 0.062, 0.080, 0.093, 0.65,cm3g−1 1.251, 1.32, 1.36 and 1.50 nm, respectively, whereas those of bare ZnCo2O4 is 0.045 cm3g-1 and 0.0746 there is a significant difference in the specific surface area of pure ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs; as a result, CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs materials exhibit significantly higher photocatalytic activity than pure ZnCo2O4. The optical absorption properties of spinel-type ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 were evaluated using UV-DRS spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4d. In contrast, the Absorption properties were determined in the 200 to 8 nm range, as shown in Fig. 4a. The ZnCo2O4 material exhibits absorption in both UV and visible light, as presented in Fig. 4d. Due to the presence of CQDs, the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs exhibit increased light absorption in the visible range compared to pure ZnCo2O4. The band gap value of the material was measured using the Tau plot expression [47]. The band gap value of ZnCO2O4 was determined to be 2.61, 2.55, 2.2, 2.10, and 2.0 eV, respectively (Fig. 4e and f). Among all the 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 have a low bandgap value, which results in increased photocatalytic activity as compared to pure and other MSs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evaluation of 0.7 CQDs ZnCo2O4 for OER
ce:section.ce:para: The OER performance of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs was conducted using a standard three-electrode system in alkaline media. Additionally, the electrochemical performance of carbon paper was also evaluated and it exhibited negligible catalytic activity, indicating that the electrochemical performance was from the synthesized materials only. From Figs. 5a and 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs exhibit an enhanced activity for OER with an overpotential of 220 mV at 100 mA cm−2 when compared to 0.5 CQDs ZnCo2O4 (360 mV), 0.2 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 (390 mV), ZnCo2O4 (700 mV), and 1 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 (310 mV) as depicted in Fig. 5b. As seen in Fig. 5c and Table S2, 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 exhibits superior OER performance when compared to previous reported work for OER in alkaline media.

The Tafel slope was evaluated to better understand the reaction kinetics of the synthesized materials during OER process. The Tafel slope of 0.7 CQDsZnCo2O4 was observed to be 30 mV dec−1, which is lower compared to 1 CQDsZnCo2O4 (61 mV dec−1), 0.5 CQDsZnCo2O4 (68 mV dec−1), 0.2 CQDsZnCo2O4 (72 mV dec−1), and CQDsZnCo2O4 (113 mV dec−1). This indicates that 0.7 CQDsZnCo2O4 exhibits faster OER kinetics, as depicted in Fig. 5d. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) displayed in Fig. 5e and S6, the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of 0.7 CQDs ZnCo2O4 heterostructure was low as compared to the other control catalysts, indicating fast transfer of electrons for OER process.

Furthermore, the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 catalyst demonstrates exceptional OER selectivity in alkaline conditions, achieving an impressive average FE value of 96.17% at current densities of 100 mA cm−2 (Fig. 5f).

Moverover, there was no change in the LSV curve of 0.7 CQDs ZnCo2O4 after 3000 cycling voltammetry (CV) tests, indicating it robust durability for OER (Fig. 5g). Additionally, the long-term chronopotentiometry of 0.7 CQDs ZnCo2O4 electrode was conducted under constant current density of 500 mA cm−2 in a 1 M KOH solution exhibiting minimal degradation after 250 h as illustrated in Fig. 5h and Table S2. This enhanced performance can be attributed to several factors, including the rapid reaction kinetics, efficient electron transfer, high intrinsic activity, and the synergistic effects resulting from the coupling of ZnCo2O4 and CQDs.

The photocatalytic activity of pure ZnCo2O4 and CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs materials was determined by monitoring the photocatalytic activity of methylene blue and Methyle orange (5 mg L−1). The concentration of MB and MO were determined by monitoring the absorbance at the highest wavelength (λ = 664 nm for MB and λ = 464 nm for MO) are used to monitor the photocatalytic degradation process; in Fig. 6 a– d the absorption band for MB slowly lowers, and the colour of the solution gradually changes until it completely disappears after 50 min. While for the MO, an irradiation time of 140 min is needed for degradation (Fig. 6b–e) in the presence of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs revealing its weaker photocatalytic activities towards the MO when compared with MB. Moreover, the digital photographs (the insets in Fig. 6a–d) visualize the color changes of MB and MO solutions from the deep color to the colorless, revealing that both MB and MO have been degraded. The results indicated that the dye degraded on the material surface. Moreover, no hypochromic change occurred during the steady decrease in absorbance at this wavelength. During the photocatalytic degradation of MB, a hypochromic alteration in its N-demethylation was detected. As a result, the photoreaction of MB with the produced photocatalyst was initiated by the cleavage of the whole conjugated chromophore. The discoloration of the MB solution and removal of the absorption band confirmed the breakdown of the chromophore structure [48].

The degrading activity of the produced photocatalyst was defined as C/Co as a function of time (Fig. 6b – e, S2 – S4), where C is the reaction concentration, and Co is the initial concentration. Additionally, the experiment was conducted in the presence and absence of the catalyst in the dark under UV light illumination. While no significant degradation occurs in the dark without the catalyst, the organic dyes are wholly degraded under UV light illumination in 50 and 140 min in the presence of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4, which has a significantly higher photocatalytic activity than pure ZnCo2O4 and other MSs. Notably, the photocatalytic performances of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 are equal to or even higher than those of previously reported photocatalysts (Fig. 4g–Table S3). As illustrated in Figs. S2 and 6c and f, MB and MO photodecomposition via pseudo-first-order kinetics (ln co/c) = kt. Compared to other photocatalysts, the 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 photocatalyst exhibits a high degradation rate. The much-improved outcome produced by combining 0.7CQDs/ZnCo2O4 highlights the vital importance of CQDs.

Photoluminescence analysis was used to investigate the photochemical characteristics of semiconductor material. Previously, high emission intensity in the Pl spectra were considered to imply a high recombination rate of photoinduced carriers [49], and this analysis successfully offers information about the charge carrier recombination process. Fig. S3 shows the pl spectra of ZnCo2o4, CQDs/ZnCo2O4, MSs excited at 365 nm and the emission wavelength around 450 nm; the lowest pl spectra of 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs shows that ZnCo2O4 was affected by CQDs; additionally, 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs display the lowest pl intensity shows the high charge separation and good photocatalytic activity [50].

The photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs under UV light irradiation is depicted in Fig. 7 . Typically, the photocatalytic performance of a catalyst is significantly influenced by the redox capabilities of its electrons and holes [51]. Additionally, the band potentials of the catalyst are crucial factors in determining its photocatalytic efficiency. The following equations [9] can be utilized for determining the conduction band minimum energy (ECBM) and valence band maximum energy (EVBM) of the CQDs/ZnnCo2O4 composite.
(1) ECB = X- E− 0.5 Eg
(2) VBM = Eg + ECB
In the given context, ECB and EVB represent the conduction and valence band edge potentials, correspondingly; Ee signifies the energy of a free electron in relation to the normal hydrogen electrode (4.5 eV vs. NHE); Eg denotes the band gap of a semiconductor; and X, which is the geometric mean of the absolute electronegativity of the constituent atoms, signifies the electronegativity of a semiconductor.

Consequently, the EVB and ECB values of CQDs and ZnCo2O4 are computed to be 2.89 and 2.416 eV (relative to NHE), corresponding to −0.31 and 0.356 eV (relative to NHE). In accordance with previous research [52], the conduction and valence band edges of ZnCo2O4 are positioned midway between those of CQDs (Fig. 7a) in the composite. Under UV light irradiation, photoelectrons can jump from the respective valence band (VB) to conduction band (CB) of CQDs and ZnCo2O4 semiconductors, owing to their narrower band gap and enhanced light absorption capability in the UV light regions. In contrast, holes remain in their respective VB. The charge migration mechanism employs the conventional approach (Fig. 7a) to illustrate the transfer of photogenerated electrons and holes from CQDs to ZnCo2O4. As a result, the recombination probability of electrons and holes is significantly enhanced, as they would be confined to the composites sole ZnCo2O4 component. This is due to the fact that the electrons accumulated on the CB of ZnCo2O4 have a higher ECB potential (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) than E (O2/•O2 −) [53], which prevents them from reducing the O2 molecules adsorbed on the catalyst surface and producing •O2 −. Additionally, only the slightly left holes on the VB of ZnCo2O4 are capable of producing reactive species of •OH, as its EVB is more positive than E (H2O/•OH, 2.27 eV vs. NHE) and E (OH‒/•OH, 1.99 eV [54]. The aforementioned analysis suggests that the incorporation of ZnCo2O4 into CQDs could potentially lead to a reduction in the concentration of reactive species and a weakening of photocatalytic activities. Nevertheless, this finding stands in stark contrast to the previously mentioned photocatalytic outcomes, as illustrated in Fig. 6.

A more careful determination of the photocatalytic mechanism was carried out by the Photocatalytic experiments in the presence of various radical scavengers, such as EDTA, l-ascorbic acid, and ter-butanol [55], as illustrated in Fig. 6 g, h for trapping photogenerated holes, superoxide anions, and hydroxyl radicals, respectively, revealed that the photocatalytic activity of the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSss dropped significantly when EDTA, l-ascorbic acid, and ter-butanol were used. This demonstrates that photogenerated electrons, holes and . OH are all active species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes.

Consequently, neither the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 composite nor the presence of the present active species in this photocatalytic process can be accounted for by the conventional charge transfer mechanism. As depicted schematically in Fig. 7b–a plausible reverse charge transfer mechanism in CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs is thus provisionally proposed. The intense electronic interaction between CQDs and ZnCo2O4 in the charge transfer mechanism is not restricted to occupied states but also extends to unoccupied levels. The negative potential exhibited by the unoccupied sites of ZnCo2O4 is greater than that of the ECB sites of CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 7b. When exposed to UV light, electrons are transferred from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) of CQDs and ZnCo2O4, respectively. With regard to the ZnCo2O4 semiconductor, the excited electrons are present in both occupied and unoccupied states. The unoccupied band potential of ZnCo2O4 is diminished in comparison to the ECB potential of CQDs. As stated in the literature [8], the establishment of a heterojunction between CQDs and ZnCo2O4 semiconductors enables the transfer of photogenerated electrons from ZnCo2O4 to CQDs until the Fermi levels of CQDs and ZnCo2O4 are equivalent. Consequently, this may lead to the buildup of negative charges in the CB region of CQDs. Concurrently, perforations are transferred from the VB of CQDs to that of ZnCo2O4. Furthermore, it is observed that the oxidative standard potential of O2/•O2‒ (−0.046 eV vs. NHE) is more positive than the CB potential of CQDs (−0.31), indicating that the electrons accumulated in the CB of CQDs have the capability to reduce O2 in order to generate active species of •O2 −. The VB potential of ZnCo2O4 is significantly higher at 2.416 eV than the reductive standard potentials of OH‒/•OH (1.99 eV vs. NHE) and H2O/•OH (2.27 eV vs. NHE) [56]. This suggests that the holes present in the VB of ZnCo2O4 have the capability to oxidize the H2O or OH− into the reactive species of •OH. In contrast, MB and MO can be oxidized directly by the VB hole of ZnCo2O4 because the redox potential of EVB (ZnCo2O4) is significantly greater than that of MB (1.08 eV) and MO (1.48 eV). To summarize, the enhanced photocatalytic activities of CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs can be attributed to the improved mobility of photogenerated electrons and holes, which is facilitated by the high interface quality at the junction.

The catalyst lifetime is a critical parameter of the photocatalytic process because its use for a more extended period results in a substantial treatment cost reduction. Four consecutive cycles were performed on the synthesized material to ensure the stability of the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 during photocatalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 6i, the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 retains good photocatalytic activity after four consecutive cycles. Additionally, after four cycles, the crystallinity of the prepared CQDs/ZnCo2O4 remained constant and did not change, as illustrated in Fig. S7. This analysis demonstrates that the CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs are not only photo-catalytically active. But also stable over time, exhibiting no visible photo erosion during the photocatalytic decomposition of MB molecules is critical for commercial applications.

In this work, a simple ultrasonication/calcination approach was used to synthesize CQDs coupled with ZnCo2O4 catalyst to employe it as a bifunctional catalyst in OER and photocatalytic degradation activity. The bifunctional CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs displayed superior OER activity in alkaline media with low overpotential of 220 mV at 100 mA cm−2 and robust durability at 250 h at 500 mA cm−2. The enhanced performance of CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs heterostructure as a bifunctional catalyst resulted from the synergistic effects within CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs, large surface area and optimized charge redistribution. Furthermore, for the MB and MO dyes, photo-catalytically increased degrading performance was observed under UV light. The optimum 0.7 CQDs/ZnCo2O4 MSs rate constant k value (0.0721 min−1) was approximately 8.0111 and 6.55 times bigger than that of pristine CQDs (0.009 min−1) and ZnCo2O4 (0.011 min−1). A potential type II reaction mechanism for improved photocatalytic degradation activity was therefore suggested. This study demonstrates a novel approach for designing and engineering high-performance, cost effective heterostructure photo/electrocatalysts for wastewater treatment and OER activity.
